data.title: Snap-On: Margins Will Keep Expanding As Vehicles Become More Complex
data.description:
Snap-on (NYSE:SNA) is definitely not a sexy stock, and you won't frequently see it on the news, but the company is as reliable as a Swiss watch. Since 1936, it has been paying quarterly cash dividends without a single decrease or pause.

If you go to a mechanic's workshop, there is a high chance you will see Snap-on's tools, and probably, you will get good comments about them from the mechanic.

And this is the reason the company caught my attention in the first place. Despite what seems an easy-to-replicate but mission-critical product, mechanics keep buying from Snap-on.

Investment Thesis

Despite operating in a mature market growing at mid-single digits, Snap-on benefits from the all-time high average age of vehicles, requiring more frequent repairs, and increasing complexity.

As vehicles include more electronic components, Snap-on's revenues from diagnostic tools and software (the highest margin segment) increase, as we saw during Q1 2025.

Snap-on will grow EPS at ~8% per year, and taking into account the 2.8% dividend yield, I expect Snap-on to deliver a 10-11% rate of return going forward.

Snap-on's Business Model

Snap-on makes money by selling tools, equipment, and diagnostic systems used to repair and maintain vehicles and infrastructure in any industry involving nuts and bolts.

The company has a market cap of $16.8B, generated $5.1B in revenues during 2024, and a free cash flow of $1.1B (expected to decline by 7% in 2025).

The Snap-on Tools Group consists of revenues generated through its 4,700 franchised van network to repair shops and mechanics.

Snap-on's franchises purchase tools from the company at a discount and have pre-established routes where they visit repair shops. Also, Snap-on receives monthly fees, although this is not a meaningful source of revenue ($19.4M in 2024 compared to almost $2B from tools sold through this channel).

The Repair Systems & Information Group's (RS&I) revenues are generated directly through Snap-on's distribution channels, and it has higher operating margins (25.7% in Q1 2025), since it doesn't have to share the profits with franchises.

The Commercial and Industrial Group has lower margins (15.5% in Q1 2025), and it serves industrial companies in the aerospace, natural resources, military, transportation, and agriculture sectors.

Finally, the Financial Services division (~70% operating margins) generates interest income by financing big-ticket purchases.

Competitive Advantage

Many investors might think of Snap-on as a manufacturer of spanners and screwdrivers, and while it is true that these are some of its products, some others are significantly more complex.

Diagnostic systems generated about 20% of Snap-on's revenues last year, and these are way stickier than screwdrivers.

Snap-on has a wide range of diagnostics tools depending on the industry, usage, and customer needs, and once a repair shop gets used to a particular product, it is unlikely it will change to a competitor.

One of Snap-on's main competitive advantages is its strong reputation and long track record of quality products.

Even with products that are easier to replicate, on many occasions, customers will prioritize buying a well-established brand over a new, less-known product.

Imagine, for example, a vehicle lift. This is a one-time spending for a repair shop (or one every 50 years), since these are long-lasting tools.

However, I don't consider Snap-on a highly defensive business, given that in an economic slowdown, some repair shops may shut down or look for a cheaper alternative.

Albeit Snap-on can charge a premium for its superior quality, brand reputation, and wide range of tools (one-stop shop), its pricing power is limited.

Solid Balance Sheet

Snap-on has a rock-solid balance sheet with zero long-term debt and $1.3B in cash.

While the company doesn't need so much cash to operate the business, and it could be returned to shareholders through a special dividend or buybacks (the stock is not trading at unreasonable levels), I believe they might be awaiting some opportunistic acquisition.

Its assets also include $0.8B in receivables and $1B in inventories. One aspect that I don't view positively is the decrease in inventory turnover, although the company has also increased its receivables payment period and managed to keep returns on capital at the same levels (~17%).

Growth Driven by Margin Expansion

To grow, Snap-on doesn't need to reinvest substantial amounts of capital in infrastructure due to its franchise model, and D&A expenses are less than 10% of its FCF.

Although it has a negative working capital and needs to increase its inventories as it grows, it benefits from operating leverage and the increasing complexity of vehicles (more on that later).

Gross margins remained flat (~51%) during the last decade, but operating margins before financial services expanded from 16.3% a decade ago to 22.7% in 2024.

Despite the modest 4% revenue growth since 2006, earnings per share have grown at a 14% CAGR due to margin expansion.

Since the repair and maintenance tools market is mature and volume growth opportunities are limited, Snap-on deploys some of its free cash flow into acquisitions to expand the business.

Capital Allocation Strategy

Snap-on has a clear capital allocation strategy. The first priority is reinvesting in the business and then closing acquisitions of complementary businesses that share the company's values.

One positive thing about Snap-on's acquisition strategy is that the risk of making an impactful mistake is limited.

The acquisitions are relatively small, and they do not experiment with new segments. During the last decade, they deployed $580M in acquisitions, which is ~8% of the net income generated during the same period.

We're not looking to transform our business. We're looking to find businesses that do what we do, and we know dramatic -- we know clearly who we are.

We are a company who doesn't make their money by the penny, makes their money in the critical where we can get a premium for solving these problems.

If we see somebody who operates in the critical, we'll consider acquiring them. But we won't go into retail, we won't venture into things like retail.

Source: Nicholas Pinchuk (CEO). Bank of America Industrials, Transportation & Airlines Key Leaders Conference May 2025.

The third and fourth priorities are dividends and buybacks. At the moment, Snap-on pays a 2.8% dividend yield and has reduced the share count by 0.7% during the last twelve months.

Tailwinds: Aging Vehicles and Increasing Complexity

Last year, the average vehicle age in the U.S. was 12.6 years, and it increased even further to 12.8 years according to an S&P Global report published a few days ago.

While many industries get negatively affected by tariffs, macro uncertainty, and inflation, Snap-on and the aftermarket industry benefit from the decline in new vehicle purchases.

Also, as vehicles include more electronic components and become more complex, fixing them requires more diagnostic tools. This not only increases revenues but is also positive from the margins perspective, since it is the highest margin segment of the business.

As the car gets more complex, the software that we have that's proprietary becomes more essential to do the repair. That's why you're seeing software goes up in RS&I.

Souce: Nicholas Pinchuk (CEO). Oppenheimer 20th Annual Industrial Growth Conference May 2025.

Expected Growth

During Q1, the only segment that grew was the RS&I (3.7% organic revenue growth), and margins improved by 140bp, driven by higher sales of diagnostic tools.

The franchise and industrial segments' revenues declined by 6.8% and 2.9%, respectively, driving Snap-on's overall margins down to 21.9% from 22.9% a year ago, and reducing EPS by 8% YoY.

The main reason for the decline has been the macro uncertainty and lower spending from repair shops due to the negative sentiment.

Following the miss on earnings during the first quarter, the full-year estimates have been revised downwards, and the EPS expectations for 2025 have been reduced from $20.17 to $18.7 (2.6% yearly decline).

Even with the recovery in consumer sentiment, I expect a slightly higher decline in EPS (-4% to -6%) compared to the analyst consensus.

Although the degree of uncertainty will be lower compared to the days following "Liberation Day" and the first tariff announcement, I don't see Snap-on's clients rushing to purchase tools that are relatively expensive.

However, it is important to take into account that unless there is a strong economic recession, the delay in tool purchases will recover in the upcoming quarters.

From a long-term perspective, I see no reason to forecast higher than historical revenue growth rates, which is in line with independent research forecasts ranging from 3.5% to 5.2% CAGR.

Assuming a 4% revenue growth, a 50bp operating margin expansion from operating leverage and faster growth from the higher margin RS&I segment, and a 0.7% decrease in share count per year, Snap-on will grow EPS at ~8% per year.

Considering the 2.8% dividend yield, I expect Snap-on to deliver a 10-11% rate of return going forward.

Valuation

Even though the expected rate of return of Snap-on's business might not be attractive enough at the moment, I consider it a quality business that could become a compelling investment if bought at a lower multiple.

If Snap-on keeps missing expectations in the upcoming quarters, the valuation could decline to the 11-12x EPS multiple range.

In that situation, the 10-11% expected return could become more attractive if we add the chance of a multiple expansion.

For now, I view Snap-on as a hold.

Risks

Snap-on has a solid balance sheet and does not pursue risky acquisitions. Overall, I do not see high disruption risks for the business model, and as long as it can keep delivering superior products at a fair price, its reputation will remain intact.

The major risks I foresee are higher raw materials prices (steel) and a tough macroeconomic environment.

A substantial percentage of its tools are made of steel, so the company's margins are sensitive to its price, as was proven in 2022 with a 130bp decline in gross margins.

The positive aspect is that the company has 10 manufacturing facilities in the U.S., so the impact of tariffs is reduced substantially, as it only needs to import some materials, but not the final product.

The other major risk is a hard recession. Although Snap-on's business is not highly cyclical, since repair shops and the rest of the industries it serves will need its mission-critical tools to keep operating, they can delay purchases in an uncertain environment.

This can be clearly seen by looking at its performance during the great financial crisis. In 2009, revenues declined by 17.2% and net income by 43%, and it took three years to recover 2008's revenues.

Bottom Line

All in all, Snap-on has an above-average business model.

The company has a strong balance sheet, attractive returns on capital, and some competitive advantages (albeit with limited pricing power).

While I expect margins to keep expanding as the average vehicle age increases along with its complexity, driving higher diagnosis tools, it operates in a mature market with low revenue growth opportunities.

At the current price, Snap-on is a hold until it decreases to a more attractive valuation ($250 per share would make it a buy).

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 Plan - Portfolio 2041-2044 Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary1 Highlights

  • In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February.
  • Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, especially related to trade and tariffs.
  • The Fed kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024.

Market review and outlook

In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February. Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs, and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Amid rising volatility, the index gained 2.78% in January before falling in February (-1.30%) and March (-5.63%). It was particularly dragged down by growth stocks and small-cap stocks.

During the quarter, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024. The central bank indicated that further reductions would depend on real progress on inflation or unexpected weakness in labor. New data showed that inflation climbed 2.80% in February from a year earlier—higher than the Fed's 2.00% target—as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

Against this backdrop, the S&P 500 Index returned -4.27% in the first quarter. However, international developed-market stocks gained 6.94%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, buoyed partly by strength in Germany (+16.00%), where February brought elections and optimism that the new administration led by Friedrich Merz would pursue a pro-growth agenda. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 2.93% advance. Some emerging European markets, including Poland (+31%), Greece (+23%), and the Czech Republic (+29%), posted strong returns, bolstered by an improved outlook for the eurozone following Germany's plans to boost spending on defense and infrastructure. China (+15%) was also a standout performer; however, Taiwan (-13%) and India (-3.00%) were relatively weak.

Investment-grade bonds rebounded during the quarter as U.S. Treasury yields eased. Long-term Treasuries and Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities did particularly well amid growing economic growth and inflation concerns. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.78%.

Almost every measure of consumer and business sentiment, or soft data, has notably deteriorated amid rising uncertainty around trade policy. While sentiment usually lags hard data, such as employment and spending, today's soft data signals a far more dire outlook than the hard data implies. The real concern is whether businesses and consumers will continue to face this level of uncertainty for a prolonged period. With trade disputes lasting for nearly a year and a half during President Trump's first term, the sentiment could be right this time around, foreshadowing a deeper economic slowdown. Given the heightened risk, we continued to lower U.S. equity exposure while adding to European equities on an upside potential related to Germany's increased fiscal spending on defense and infrastructure and a more unified commitment from leaders across the eurozone on improving competitiveness.

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a slight loss for the quarter but outpaced its blended benchmark, comprising a 70% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index and a 30% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net). An out-of-benchmark stake in the Real Assets Fund (T. Rowe Price) notably contributed for the quarter, as this asset class outperformed core equities, driven by the precious metals and energy groups. Exposure to non-U.S. stocks also added value in a period during which U.S. equities lagged. International Growth Fund (John Hancock) was a key relative contributor, aided by security selection in the industrials sector. Equity Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) also provided a relative performance boost, largely on the strength of security selection within industrials and consumer staples. On the other hand, Disciplined Value Fund (John Hancock) detracted, mainly due to stock selection in information technology, communication services, and financials.

EXPENSE RATIOS TOTAL
Class A 1.23%

Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance current to the most recent month end is available at 529 - Manulife John Hancock Investments. Diversification cannot assure a profit or protect against loss in a declining market.

Note: performance for periods prior to 11/14/2019 reflects the prior maximum sales charge and expenses of the portfolios. Please see the Plan Disclosure Document for more details.

The performance of the enrollment-based portfolios reflects changes in asset allocations over time relating to the targeted enrollment date of Beneficiaries for which the particular Investment Option is designed.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. This commentary is provided for informational purposes only and is not an endorsement of any security, mutual fund, sector, or index. The subadvisor, the advisor (John Hancock Investment Management LLC), and their affiliates, employees, and clients, may hold or trade the securities mentioned in this commentary. Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of a loss.

Portfolio 2041-2044 Managed by T.Rowe Price

Long-tenured manager offering expertise across asset classes and a risk-aware style of investing built on fundamental, in-house research on a global scale

On the fund since 2023. Investing since 2001

What you should know before investing

Investing involves risks, including the potential loss of principal. There is no guarantee that a portfolio's investment strategy will be successful or that education expenses will be met. Even if you contribute the maximum amount, there is no assurance that the money in your account will be sufficient to cover all the education expenses your beneficiary may incur or that the rate of return on your investment will match or exceed the rate at which education expenses may rise. The impact of inflation on education expenses is uncertain and could exceed the return on investments in your account. Please see the Plan Disclosure Document for additional risks.

Portfolio 2041-2044 Blended Benchmark: MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. Net Index, 30.00%; Russell 3000 Index, 70.00% The MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) ex-U.S. Net Index tracks the performance of publicly traded large- and mid-cap stocks of companies in 22 developed markets and 23 emerging markets. The Russell 3000 Index tracks the performance of 3,000 publicly traded large-, mid-, and small-cap companies in the United States. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

If your state or your designated beneficiary's state offers a 529 plan, you may want to consider what, if any, potential state income-tax or other state benefits it offers, such as financial aid, scholarship funds, and protection from creditors, before investing. State tax or other benefits should be one of many factors to be considered prior to making an investment decision. Please consult with your financial, tax, or other financial professional about how these state benefits, if any, may apply to your specific circumstances. You may also contact your state 529 plan or any other 529 education savings plan to learn more about their features. Please contact your financial professional or call 866-222-7498 to obtain a Plan Disclosure Document or prospectus for any of the underlying funds. The Plan Disclosure Document contains complete details on investment objectives, risks, fees, charges, and expenses, as well as more information about municipal fund securities and the underlying investment companies that should be considered before investing. Please read the Plan Disclosure Document carefully prior to investing.

● Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, especially related to trade and tariffs.

● The Fed kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024.

Market review and outlook

data.title: Uranium Energy Corp: A Strategic Bet On Nuclear's Resurgence?
data.description:
Macro-Economic Overview: Uranium Market Dynamics

Global interest in uranium is rising again, fueled by a combination of key trends: the push for energy independence, stricter climate goals, and a growing shift back toward nuclear power as a steady, emissions-free source of electricity.

According to the World Nuclear Association, over 70 nuclear reactors are under construction across 17 nations with around 100 in advanced planning. A further 300 projects across the globe are on the drawing board, reflecting growing momentum behind nuclear expansion.

This next wave of nuclear build-out is led by China, which plans to add 150 new reactors by 2035, alongside India, Russia, and Middle Eastern nations such as the UAE and Saudi Arabia. Even in OECD countries, there is a revival: Japan has restarted 12 reactors, France has extended lifespans for 32 of its 56 reactors, and the U.S. has pledged to support small modular reactor development under its Inflation Reduction Act.

Growing demand in uranium has led to a structural supply deficit that is projected to continue widening.

On the supply front, uranium markets remain structurally undersupplied. In 2022, global mine production totaled roughly 58,000 metric tonnes of U₃O₈—equivalent to about 152 million pounds—while demand from nuclear reactors exceeded 65,000 tonnes, or 170 million pounds. The gap between supply and demand has so far been bridged only partially by secondary sources such as stockpile drawdowns and recycled materials.

Analysts expect uranium production to rise by close to 12% in 2024, pushing global output beyond 60,000 tonnes. Kazakhstan and Canada represent the lion's share of this increase in output.

Despite this, key players including Cameco and Kazatomprom have maintained a policy of limited production. For example, Cameco mined only ~18 million pounds in 2023, well below its full operating capacity of 30 million pounds. Political volatility in uranium-exporting countries such as Niger (which accounts for ~4% of global output), as well as broader uncertainty in Russia and Kazakhstan—which together represent about 40% of global supply—continues to cloud the outlook for reliable sourcing.

Cost structures vary measurably across the sector. In-situ recovery (ISR) operations in the U.S. and Kazakhstan are relatively variable but offer a clear advantage over conventional mines. Uranium spot prices have risen sharply, recently approaching $70 per pound, while long-term contract pricing presently holding near $80 per pound.

Against this backdrop, companies with permitted ISR assets and minimal capital requirements—such as Uranium Energy Corp (NYSE:UEC)—are increasingly well-positioned to benefit from tightening supply and elevated prices. Large-scale greenfield projects, on the other hand, continue to face long permitting timelines and capital expenditures that frequently top $1 billion, making near-term producers even more appealing to investors.

Company Overview: Uranium Energy Corp (UEC)

Founded in 2005 and headquartered in Corpus Christi, Texas, Uranium Energy Corp is a leading U.S.-based uranium mining and exploration company. UEC has adopted a dual strategy:

  1. Production-Ready ISR Portfolio: Operating in Texas and Wyoming, UEC controls several ISR projects capable of near-term, low-cost uranium production.
  2. Physical Uranium Ownership: UEC holds over 1.35 million lbs of physical uranium, offering a strategic inventory to capitalize on price surges.

Uranium Energy Corp remains one of America’s fastest growing Uranium producers.

Performance Metrics and Financial Overview

Currently, Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC) boasts a market capitalization of approximately $2.8 billion with valuation reflecting investor confidence in the firm's well-defined, long-term strategic positioning. While UEC has kicked off a carefully phased ramp-up of its U.S. uranium production, a central tenet of its strategy remains the methodical build-up of physical inventory. This approach, coupled with a commitment to preserving operational flexibility, means that near-term revenue contributions are intentionally modest. Uranium Energy Corp's intrinsic value is thus more closely tied to its strategic optionality and its leveraged exposure to appreciating uranium prices.

Underscoring its strategic approach, UEC maintains a robust financial profile. The company boasts approximately $214 million in cash and liquid assets and notably carries no long-term debt. This prudent capital structure minimizes financial risk and affords management the latitude for strategic decision-making, free from pressing financing concerns. Furthermore, this debt-free stance fosters strong alignment with shareholder interests and equips Uranium Energy Corp. to rapidly intensify production activities as soon as market conditions signal an opportune moment.

Uranium Energy Corp maintains four production growth pillars along with an extensive exploration portfolio.

Nearly 75% of Uranium Energy Corp.’s shares are held by large institutional investors, including well-known names like BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street. For individual investors, this level of backing can be a strong vote of confidence in both the management team and the direction UEC is heading.

Liquidity and Valuation

Uranium Energy Corp (NYSE:UEC) is currently trading at approximately $6.50 per share as of May 2025, and yields a price-to-book (P/B) ratio of ~3.0. While this indicates positive market sentiment toward the company’s asset base and uranium strategy, reflecting a discount compared to several of its uranium mining peers.

  • Cameco Corp (CCJ), the sector heavyweight, trades at a P/B of ~5.8, underscoring its role as a mature, cash-generating producer.
  • Denison Mines (DNN) and NexGen Energy (NXE) both trade at P/B multiples of ~4.1 and ~4.7, respectively, reflecting market optimism around their development pipelines.
  • By contrast, Energy Fuels (UUUU) and Paladin Energy (PDN.AX) are valued at P/B ratios closer to 2.0 and 1.7, respectively.

Although UEC isn't the lowest-priced stock in its group, the firm remains competitively priced against a basket of peers. As production milestones are gradually met or uranium prices continue to strengthen, it's likely the firm benefits from a potential re-rating to the upside.

UEC is heavily traded, with more than 30 million shares bought and sold on average. This puts it near the top of the list when it comes to activity among uranium-focused stocks in North America. It falls under the Energy – Uranium sector, a space that’s drawn increased attention from investors in 2024 and 2025 as nuclear power gains momentum again.

Year-to-date, UEC has delivered a total return of approximately +28% (as of May 27, 2025), outperforming both the S&P 500 and the Global X Uranium ETF (URA), which are up ~11% and ~16% YTD, respectively. The next scheduled earnings release is anticipated in June 2025, as the company reports on strategic progress rather than revenue generation, given its pre-production status.

Uranium Energy Corp continues its rebound following a bout a tariff-induced volatility.

Analyst Estimates

Uranium Energy Corp (NYSE:UEC) enjoys a broadly bullish analyst consensus, with most firms rating it “Buy” or “Outperform” This optimism centers on three factors:

  1. Strategic U.S.-based ISR assets in Texas and Wyoming offer low-cost, geopolitically secure production potential (UEC Projects).
  2. A debt-free balance sheet and ~$214 million in cash provide financial flexibility (SEC Filings).
  3. Strong leverage to uranium prices, via over 5 million lbs of physical inventory and near-term production assets, positions UEC to benefit as spot uranium trades near $85/lb.

Together, these fundamentals underpin UEC’s appeal as a high-torque play on uranium’s structural bull market.

Peer Group Analysis

UEC stands out for its U.S. focus and strategic inventory positioning, compared to peers operating in geopolitically riskier regions.

Current Valuation and Optionality

Uranium Energy Corp (NYSE:UEC) presents a compelling valuation narrative built not on current revenues, but on strategic assets, financial discipline, and asymmetric upside to rising uranium prices. At the core of its valuation are three pillars:

  1. A physical uranium inventory valued at approximately $425 million, offering direct exposure to spot price movements and functioning as a liquid, appreciating asset base (UEC Investor Presentation).
  2. A portfolio of near-term ISR (In-Situ Recovery) production assets in the U.S., which are cost-effective and environmentally efficient, positioning the company for rapid cash flow generation once production is initiated.
  3. A debt-free balance sheet, backed by roughly $120 million in cash, giving UEC the financial flexibility to develop assets or act opportunistically without the dilution or interest burdens that encumber many peers (SEC Filings).

Given its pre-revenue status, conventional valuation metrics like price-to-earnings (P/E) are not applicable. Instead, the market views UEC as a leveraged option on the uranium bull cycle—with upside rooted in both physical inventory and embedded production potential.

A conservative sum-of-the-parts valuation reinforces this view:

  • Physical uranium stockpile: $425 million
  • ISR project portfolio (NPV based on $75/lb uranium): ~$1.0 to $1.5 billion
  • Strategic U.S. asset premium: $250 to $500 million

All in, these factors suggest there’s room for the stock to move higher—especially if uranium prices keep climbing or global tensions make U.S.-sourced supply more valuable.

Risk Profile: Strategic Optionality, But Not Without Exposure

Despite Uranium Energy Corp's (NYSE:UEC) promising upside, it's important for investors to keep a few key risks in mind.

  1. The company currently generates no operating revenue. While its ISR assets are close to being production-ready, any set backs in permitting, rising costs, or operational hurdles could slow down progress and cash flow.
  2. Uranium prices are highly cyclical and influenced by policy changes, geopolitical developments, and utility demand cycles. A drop in prices could make UEC's inventory and development plans less attractive.
  3. Regulatory and ESG risks loom large—particularly in the U.S. Uranium mining attracts tight oversight and community pushback may delay or derail projects.
  4. UES may need to fund capital expenditures through stock issuance, raising the prospect of shareholder dilution if alternative funding sources are limited.

These risks are common across early-stage uranium companies and highlight why careful financial planning and solid execution will be key to realizing long-term value.

Investment Case: High Torque Uranium Exposure

Uranium Energy Corp (NYSE:UEC) is a high-conviction bet on the global nuclear revival. With U.S.-based ISR assets, a $425M uranium stockpile, zero debt, and strong institutional backing, it offers rare exposure to both physical and future uranium upside. While speculative, UEC is built for investors seeking high-beta leverage to a tightening uranium market—with limited geopolitical baggage.

References:

World Nuclear Association – Reactor Database Details on current and planned global nuclear reactors

Trading Economics – Uranium Prices Historical and live uranium spot price charts

Uranium Energy Corp – Investor Presentations (Q1 2025) Official presentation deck including project updates, inventory details, and financial overview

U.S. Energy Information Administration – U.S. Nuclear Industry Overview of the U.S. nuclear fleet and interactive maps of reactor locations

data.title: Apple: China Sales Collapse Is Only Getting Worse
data.description:
Ever since I highlighted the latest China-related problems that Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) faces back in December, the company’s stock has depreciated by ~20% and underperformed against the broader market. Since that time, Apple’s troubles in China have only worsened, as its latest earnings report showed that its sales there deteriorated and the company lost market share to the domestic competitors.

At the same time, Apple’s exposure to the China-centered supply chain remains one of its major weaknesses, as the trade policy of the Trump administration might negatively affect the company’s performance and undermine its growth opportunities in the following months. The good news, though, is that the expected continuous growth of the services business might help Apple minimize the risks that it faces.

This Doesn’t Look Good

A few weeks ago, Apple reported fairly modest Q2 earnings results as its revenues increased by 6% Y/Y to $95.4 billion and were above expectations by $840 million. Its GAAP EPS of $1.65 was also above the expectations by $0.03.

While the overall business managed to grow, the problem is that the Greater China sales in Q2 were down by 2% Y/Y to $16 billion and below the expectations of $16.83 billion. In the first six months of the fiscal year, the sales also declined by 7% Y/Y to $34.52 billion.

One of the biggest reasons behind such an underperformance in China is the inability to launch the company’s AI features on iPhones in the country. Despite planning the release of Apple Intelligence for April, the launch has been delayed, and currently, no new date has been announced by the company.

At the same time, American officials began to question Apple’s decision to work with Alibaba (BABA) on bringing Apple Intelligence to China. If the American government decides to prevent Alibaba from doing business with U.S. companies, then such an action might prevent Apple from releasing the AI features for iPhones in one of the most important consumer markets in the world anytime soon. Because of all of that, Apple has now lost its dominant position in the Chinese smartphone market and is currently the fifth most popular phone manufacturer, with a market share of only 13.7%, down from 15.6% a year ago.

More importantly, Apple’s exposure to the China-centered supply chain is another major issue that will be hard to overcome anytime soon. At the end of last year, most of Apple’s suppliers produced their goods in China, while the New York Times this April reported that the company still makes around 90% of all iPhones in the country. Considering that President Trump threatened last week to impose new tariffs on smartphones that are not built in the United States by the end of June, it’s hard to see how Apple will be able to mitigate this risk. It’s almost impossible for Apple to start producing smartphones in the United States in the foreseeable future. That is why there’s a possibility that the performance of the iPhone business might suffer in the upcoming quarters if tariffs go into effect.

Considering that Apple already received dozens of downward revisions in recent months and its future looks uncertain because of the developments described above, the upside to its shares is likely to be minimal at this point.

The Recovery Is Still Possible

There are still ways for Apple to overcome some of the challenges that it faces. While iPhone sales might take a hit in the following quarters, the company has the ability to offset some of the downside caused by it thanks to the solid performance of its services business. In Q2, the services business already achieved a new sales record by generating $26.6 billion in revenues, up 12% Y/Y, and expanding in every geographic segment. The gross margin of the services business has also expanded by 70 bps Q/Q to 75.7%. Considering that Apple has over 1 billion paid subscriptions, the company’s services business is likely going to help mitigate the downside caused by the underperformance of other businesses given its impressive margins and scale.

At the same time, the macroeconomic environment might improve in the upcoming months. We already saw consumer confidence improving in May, which could indicate that consumers are more optimistic about the future. At the same time, considering the constant change in trade rhetoric by the Trump administration, there’s a possibility that the smartphone tariffs will be delayed. After all, this already happened with the auto tariffs that were eased to give automakers more time to optimize their supply chains. If that’s the case, then Apple will be able to mitigate some of the macro risks in the short to near term and boost its sales in the United States.

What’s Next for Apple’s Shares?

My discounted cash flow model from last year showed that Apple’s fair value is $190.67 per share. Apple’s stock briefly reached that level in early April after the announcement of initial reciprocal tariffs. However, once the 90-day pause was issued, the stock rebounded and is currently trading at around $200 per share. Considering that major developments occurred since the time when that model was published, I decided to update the model, which can be seen below.

The biggest change in the model happened to the revenue assumptions, as the growth rate decreased primarily in FY25 and FY26 due to the relatively weak performance in the first two quarters of the year and the deteriorating situation in China. The revenue growth rate is now lower than before, but also not that far away from the overall expectations.

At the same time, I still expect EBIT to gradually grow in the following years since the services business will likely expand even more, offset some of the risks associated with the iPhone business, and improve the overall margins. The tax rate in the model is 16%, in-line with the management’s latest expectations. The assumptions for the remaining metrics closely align with Apple’s historical performance.

The terminal growth rate in the model remains at 3%, while the weighted average cost of capital also remains at 8%, which is close to the market’s average cost of capital rate.

The updated model shows that Apple’s fair value is $180.27 per share, lower than before primarily because of the lower revenue assumptions as a result of rising troubles in China.

The Bottom Line

Going forward, it’s likely that Apple will continue to lose market share in China, which will affect iPhone sales the most in the upcoming quarters. At the same time, the expected continuous growth of the services business has a chance to minimize some of the downside caused by the weakness in China and the rising macro risks. That is why at this point, Apple’s stock is a Hold for me since the company is also trading relatively close to its fair value.

data.title: Clipper Realty: 141 Livingston Renewal, 9.8% Yield, Multifamily Rent Growth
data.description:
Brooklyn-based multifamily REIT Clipper Realty Inc. (NYSE:CLPR) currently trades at its cheapest ever valuation, with dual beats on its fiscal 2025 first quarter earnings pairing with common shares that are down roughly 16% year-to-date. CLPR reported record fiscal 2025 first quarter revenue of $39.4 million, up 10% over its year-ago comp on the back of strong rental demand supporting a 15% positive spread on new leases and around 8% on renewals.

Vacancy rates in New York are extremely low, coming in at 1.4% as of October 2024. This provides an extremely strong backdrop for 953 Dean Street, a 9-story Brooklyn residential building with roughly 160,000 square feet of residential rental gross leasable area ("GLA") and another 9,000 square feet of retail rental GLA. The property is beautiful and fully amenitized, with leasing set to commence next month from the 1st of June. It will provide 240 units with 70% of this free market.

The pending stabilization of the property comes as CLPR has agreed with the existing tenant of 141 Livingston to renew the lease for another 5 years. However, the REIT's office building at 250 Livingston is set to see its anchor tenant, 93% of the property's tenancy, exit the lease from August.

141 Livingston Vacate, Liquidity, Debt Maturities, And Free Cash Flow

The pending large tenant vacate at 250 Livingston would mean a $4.25 million revenue drop a quarter. This would have seen CLPR's quarterly revenue fall by 10.8% to $35.15 million in a scenario where 953 Dean Street was still in development far beyond the pending vacate date. The extension at 141 Livingston represents a significant win for CLPR, as the loss of the tenant at both properties would have formed a substantial headwind. Critically, continued positive spreads on both new and renewal leases and 953 Dean Street leases coming online will form a critical revenue uplift for CLPR as the REIT continues to look for new tenants for 141 Livingston.

I do not see this as posing a risk to the dividend. CLPR's main headwind will be managing the debt associated with the property with a $100 million mortgage priced at a 3.21% interest rate. The mortgage is the cheapest on CLPR's capital stack and is set to mature in the summer of 2031.

CLPR's total debt stood at $1.28 billion as of the end of the first quarter, but this is set to decline following as 10 West 65th Street in Manhattan is set to sell for $45.5 million. The sale should generate net cash proceeds of $12 million after the full repayment of the entire outstanding mortgage balance. It means CLPR will expand its cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash position which stood at $39.1 million as of the end of the first quarter. The REIT generated free cash flow of $6.68 million during the quarter, providing 152% coverage for the dividend payments. CLPR last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.0950 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution and $0.38 per share annualized for a 9.3% dividend yield.

The REIT was also able to complete new financing for 953 Dean Street with a new $160 million two-year bridge loan that repaid the existing construction loan. CLPR was able to close this at a cheaper rate of 2.65% + SOFR. The new bridge loan will also provide an extra $18 million in working capital to further bolster the REIT's liquidity position. CLPR faces $81.57 million in debt tied to 1010 Pacific Street coming due for repayment in 2025. The REIT should be able to refinance this on the back of the transaction for 953 Dean Street.

They're both high-quality properties with strong underlying demand in one of the tightest rental markets in the U.S. CLPR's debt is relatively front-loaded, ramping up its credit risk profile. This high loan balance represents a core risk for the bulls, as the REIT will have to refinance against a Fed funds rate that remains high at 4.25% to 4.50%.

The dividend yield seems elevated against CLPR's underlying fundamentals, with the REIT currently trading hands for a 5.08x multiple to its annualized first quarter funds from operations, or FFO. First quarter adjusted FFO was $0.19 per share, around $8 million, and grew from $0.14 per share a year ago.

I'm a buyer of the Clipper Realty Inc. common shares at these levels, with the risk posed by 141 Livingston vacate seemingly reflected in the current stock price. The dividend is covered by free cash flow with 953 Dean Street set to significantly improve this coverage and offset a possible revenue drop if a new tenant for 141 Livingston can't be found ahead of August.

data.title: SHYD - Perfectly Adequate, Just Not Good Enough I Think
data.description:
If we know the what and the why, we can work out whether

Many financial instruments are designed to gain a particular result, or to exploit a particular quirk of the market and or investor desires.

It therefore pays for us to work out what that design is all about, which quirk is being exploited, or which desire potentially sated. We can then decide - once we know - whether that's something we wish to do.

Yes, of course, at one level this is simply obvious. But it's astonishing how many don't go through this process before investing. More should.

VanEck Short High Yield Muni ETF (BATS:SHYD)

So, as an exchange traded fund this is an open ended fund. This means no leverage (unlike most mutual bond funds). This also means that it will trade within basis points of net asset value nearly all the time.

This also means that - potentially at least - it could be subject to a redemption cascade. So some people decide they'd like their money back and sell out. That means, after the usual cash margin kept for such things, that the fund must sell some holdings. OK, if the fund's in liquid underlyings then they do that and pay. But what if the fund's in something illiquid?

This has actually happened to a couple of open ended commercial property funds and a small company/private equity one in London. They have to do emergency sales to pay out to investors that are leaving, those fire sale prices spook more investors to redeem and we get a downward spiral - a redemption cascade.

This fund is aware of this:

You only talk about liquidity when you think liquidity is important.

Now, I think the possibility of a redemption cascade in a mutual bond operation is really pretty remote. Very remote. But it is worth mentioning that they themselves agree that they're in at least some stuff illiquid enough that it's worth being reassured about it.

Bonds

It's a bond fund and there are those three types of bonds. Treasuries, which is lending to the US Govt. These are considered to be credit risk free - they're not going to go bust. Well, they might, other governments have after all, but the possibility is very remote. Also, if it happens, we've all got far greater problems than mere financial markets.

Corporates are lending to corporations and they can go bust - that exposes us to credit risk. That people we've lent money to simply won't repay it.

If we're exposed to credit risk then we need to be diversified. So that any one bankruptcy only affects a small portion of our overall position. Corporates also pay a higher coupon (interest rate) to cover for this risk.

The problem with diversification is that it is possible to buy into corporate bonds in the $1,000 a position sort of level. But that's usually only at issue, and then to sell at that size at redemption. This isn't true of all bond markets, always, but the European corporate bond markets often have a $200k par value minimum for trading in the secondary market. It therefore costs a lot to build a diversified portfolio. This means that for us, as retail investors, buying into a fund if we're going to gain that desired, even necessary, diversification.

Municipals are lending to sub-national government bodies in the US. They have credit risk. True, it's pretty low:

... as muni bond default rates are incredibly low. As per Moody's, default rates for non-investment grade bonds average around 0.10% per year, an extremely low, figure. For investment-grade bonds, default rates are effectively zero.

And, yes, but as is always said, past performance is no proof of future performance. Munis can go bust and therefore do carry credit risk. Therefore diversification and therefore, for us retail types, a fund or an ETF.

Interest rate risk

All bonds are also subject to interest rate risk. If underlying interest rates change (E.g. the Fed Funds Rate) then the value of all extant bonds also changes. For now everyone's premium to FFR is based upon a different FFR. Last week we could get 3% (or 5%) on a new bond, this week it's 4%. All those bonds from last week now change in value by the net present value of that different coupon/interest stream they're going to receive over how long they last.

How long they last is called the "tenor". The longer the bond the greater the capital value or price change given an interest rate change. Losing (or making) 1% on a 30-day note is different from doing so on a 30-year bond.

At which point we meet a sadness in the bond market. Which is that longer tenor bonds pay a higher coupon/interest rate than shorter. If we want to gain income then we've got to take that interest rate risk. There are variations, sure, gradations, but the basic point stands.

Munis and tax equivalent yields

All of the above tells us that munis should pay a higher coupon than Treasuries, and possibly a lower one than corporates. This is not true. They pay a lower rate than Treasuries. The reason for this is that the income from them is free of the Federal income tax. At which point we can work out what is the value of the interest they do pay as compared to something else that we could have but we'd have to pay tax upon? This is called the tax equivalent yield.

This is complicated because of course it depends upon the other income you've got which is also subject to income tax. Fortunately, people prepare tables for this:

It's necessary for each individual to actually check this. But as a rule of thumb it is generally thought that if your marginal income tax rate is 24% or above then munis might be a thing for you. If lower, then perhaps not - and if you're a foreigner, or aren't paying income tax (or are in a tax protected account) then no, don't.

We can see this for this fund here:

We can get around 5% yield in USFR with pretty much zero credit or interest rate risk. That 24% rule of thumb cut off point isn't that bad as a guide.

SHYD again

So, balancing these different parts. SHYD is in the medium to short end of the muni bond market. We have the benefits of the tax break, we're at the lower end of the interest rate risk problem. We're also at the low credit rating end of the muni market, so we are maximising credit risk in the hunt for income.

Well, OK, it's a tactic.

No, that's not being merely dismissive. It's wholly legitimate to go to lower credit ratings to try to boost income. Just as we can vary across tenor to change coupons. No, it's fine as a tactic, what matters is how well it works.

Financial details

The things we like to know about every fund or ETF:

That's actually pretty concentrated. Very concentrated in fact. We get a different listing here. But that's showing issue, not issuer. That chart is showing exposure to an issuer, not an issue.

But, given muni default rates that's fine. The management fee's 0.35% which is pretty good for an actively managed fund.

But what matters is how well does this all work out? In one sense, not all that well:

Consistently not meeting the performance of the benchmark is not good. But of course there's always that next year in Jerusalem thought to keep us happy.

But my real thought here is that we can be elsewhere and gain that better yield. BYM is in munis and offers 6.29% at present. There's the already mentioned USFR which offers a better net, at lower credit and interest rate risk, performance for lower rate taxpayers.

Why I'm wrong

In the description of the market and the construction of the fund I'm not. It's also a perfectly fine attempt to balance tenor and income.

I could be wrong in not having grasped some finer point about the risk/reward ratio but I don't think I am.

My opinion

As I say, I don't think there's anything wrong here. Not beating the benchmark is disappointing of course but that's not quite the point.

My suspicion/opinion is that reaching down the credit ratings rankings in pursuit of income is something that just doesn't work in the muni market. The default rates are so low that there's really not all that much of an interest rate difference to pursue.

Yes, a muni fund has considerable advantages for higher rate taxpayers. The only problem I can really see with SHYD is that others are playing that game better. It's possible to gain higher yields elsewhere with no apparent increase in risk. So, as rational investors we should be elsewhere.

data.title: Lam Research: I Love The Innovation, But Dislike The Stock Valuation
data.description:
Lam Research Corporation (NASDAQ:LRCX) is a leading provider of etch and deposition equipment—core technologies enabling the production of the world’s most advanced semiconductors. With rising demand for high-performance chips such as AI accelerators and memory, the complexity of fabrication is growing rapidly. While chip manufacturing has never been as simple as printing designs onto silicon wafers, the need for cutting-edge process tools that enable breakthroughs in efficiency and scaling is now more important than ever.

In this report, I analyze the fiscal Q3 2025 earnings and evaluate how the company is positioned to capitalize on structural growth driven by AI integration and autonomous technologies—making it a compelling long-term investment in the future of semiconductor manufacturing, but a hold in the near term.

What Are The Opportunities And Risks For Lam Research?

To understand the opportunities for the company, we first need to understand what the company actually does. This can already be rather complex to understand. The company provides process tools and materials for precision control at the atomic scale. Deposition processes create layers of insulating and conducting materials to build semiconductors. Depending on the material and the structure, several techniques can be utilized. Lam Research provides the film disposition products critical for this. In the etch process, materials and films deposited on the wafer are removed for which Lam Research provides plasma etch systems.

The company also provides strip and clean techniques to clear the wafers surface from contamination in between manufacturing steps. So, Lam Research provides the tools for the wafer deposition, etching and strip and clean steps. These tools are integrated the process flow for foundries. The company also benefits from installed base management and training opportunities.

The major risk for Lam Research is quite obvious. A reduction in chip demand or slower growth would also affect demand for the company’s products. Furthermore, trade turmoil may affect the company’s sales to China. In the most recent quarter, China accounted for 31% of all sales.

Lam Research Sales And Margin Expansion Drive Double-Digit Earnings Growth

In Q3 2025, sales increased by nearly 8% sequentially to $4.72 billion, beating analyst estimates by almost $80 million. So far, the company has not seen any changes in custom behavior stemming from the tariffs, which I believe is a positive. Any impact to the business are expected to be manageable though the indirect impact on chip demand is obviously a different ball game. Margins improved from 47.5% to 49% driven by a stronger sales mix. Operating expenses increased by $28 million driven by higher R&D activities. Operating margins increased from 30.7% to 32.8%, indicating 15% growth. So, overall, it was a good quarter for the company with high single digit growth which coupled with margin expansion drove mid-teens growth in operating income.

Lam Research Guides For $5 Billion In Sales

For the June quarter, sales are expected to be between $4.7 billion and $5.3 billion marking a 6% sequential increase in sales. Margins are guided at 49.5% with non-GAAP operating margins are expected to be 33.5%. Both margins could vary by 100 basis points in either direction. Earnings per share are expected to be between $1.10 and $1.30.

Lam Research Stock Is A Hold

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

In some way, Lam Research gives me mixed feelings. I like the innovative spirit of the company. However, if we connect the forecasts to the valuation all of a sudden there is a whole lot less to be positive about. Compounded annual growth rate for EBITDA is just 5.7% through 2027 and 3.8% for free cash flow. The company trades above its median valuation and while surging demand for semiconductor equipment may justify this, we also see that the stock has the FY25 and FY26 earnings priced in already. There is 20% upside against FY27 earnings, but we are also mindful about the trade risk. As a result, I am marking the stock a hold with a $97.01 price target based on a peer group valuation.

Conclusion: Lam Research Stock Is Not Attractive Yet

While I like Lam Research Corporation’s low leverage and its innovative spirt and support in semiconductor manufacturing, the valuation looks a whole lot less attractive to me at this stage, and I would welcome a pullback in the stock price to create a more compelling entry point. At current price levels and with the trade risks in mind, I do not believe that the current stock price offers a compelling entry point.

data.title: Booking Holdings: Quietly Killing The Competition And Crushing The Market
data.description:
As hot topics like AI, tariffs, inflation, and budget deficits, suck all the attention and take all the headlines, Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG) has been quietly one of the top performers in the market.

Somehow, while peers like Airbnb (ABNB) are searching for identity, and Expedia (EXPE) is struggling to build its international brand, Booking Holdings continues to silence the doubters with quarter after quarter of better-than-expected growth.

With shares up 12% year-to-date and 46% over the past year, is there still meat left on the bone? Let's find out.

Revisiting The Booking Holdings Investment Thesis

People like to travel. They just do. And, contrary to what many argue, travel is not the first thing that gets crossed off the budget list during tougher times. In fact, for many families, the highest priority item on the discretionary list is travel. That is a key reason for the travel industry's historical growth rates, which significantly exceed global GDP.

The industry is divided into aggregators, like Booking, Expedia, or Airbnb, and providers, like Hilton (HLT), or Marriott (MAR). In some sense, Booking is to travel what DoorDash (DASH) is to food.

As such, Booking is arguably the safest way to capitalize on the growth of the entire industry. It operates across the entire value chain, with both hotels and alternative accommodation offerings, as well as transportation and experiences. And, most importantly, it is the dominant global player.

So, Booking is much less sensitive to changes in preferences, trading down, or specific geographic risks. And, as it turns out, it is the perfect place to be:

Over the past five years, Booking has returned 164%, higher than any of its peers, and more than double the market. It has done so as it reached record scale, but has yet to recapture its pre-pandemic margins, partially because of a change in its business model (Booking now facilitates payments directly).

From quarter to quarter, Booking continues to prove its underrated. The last quarter was no different.

Extraordinary First Quarter & Ambitious Guidance Despite Uncertainty

Revenue in the first quarter was $4.8 billion, up 8% Y/Y, about $200 million ahead of estimates and company guidance. This marked a deceleration from the 14% growth last quarter, although it came on the back of very tough comps, as Booking grew 17% in Q1-24.

Growth was primarily driven by Room Nights, which were up 7% Y/Y, led by Europe and Asia, both growing high-single-digits, followed by rest of world and the US, both at low-single-digits. The remainder ~1% was driven by increased payments penetration and higher average daily rates, or ADRs.

Looking at Gross Bookings, we see 7% growth, slower than the 17% in the previous quarter and the 10% in the prior year period. Growth in bookings benefitted from ADR growth, as well as strong flight bookings.

So, all growth items point to a high-single-digit pace, in line with what Booking views as the normalized long-term growth rate. Supposedly, despite all the noise, we might have reached normal levels in the travel industry.

Meanwhile, Booking continues to improve its margin profile, and reached record margins for a March quarter in the post-pandemic era. Biggest improvement factors were Sales, Personnel, and G&A. The better margins, combined with another quarter of aggressive buybacks, drove a 22% increase in adjusted EPS.

Another Quarter Of Market Share Gains For The Travel Leader

In concentrated portfolios, investors should always look to invest in the most attractive, and the best-of-breed company, in their industry of choice. In travel, I think Booking is the undeniable leader.

Despite being the largest, by far, Booking consistently outgrows its peers, reflecting the value of network effects and brand, as well as the simple notion that it provides the best service.

In the first quarter, Booking had a 45.5% share of the combined gross bookings between itself, Expedia, and Airbnb, up 40 bps Y/Y. Despite being almost 50% larger than Expedia and nearly twice as big as Airbnb, Booking achieved the fastest growth rate at 7.2%, compared to Airbnb's 7.0% and Expedia's 4.3%.

Perhaps the biggest flex, is that Booking is getting the most bookings, and has the most listings on its marketplace, despite charging a higher take rate from the providers. Again, it is doing that and growing the fastest.

Even in flights, which used to be Expedia's field, Booking has now claimed the top spot, growing 46% last quarter, compared to Expedia's 4%. Booking booked a total of 16 million flights in the first quarter, an all-time high for the company.

Similarly, Booking has been crushing Airbnb's growth rates in Alternative Accommodations, which is growing faster than total room nights on Booking. Last quarter, Booking Alternative Room Nights rose 12% Y/Y, compared to Airbnb's 7.9%.

The bottom line is, everywhere you look, Booking is winning.

Valuation & Prospects

Booking expects this year's results to be roughly in line with their long-term targets, of high-single-digit bookings and revenue growth, faster EBITDA growth driven by margin expansion, and mid-teens EPS growth through aggressive buybacks.

The upcoming second quarter is expected to exceed the full-year guidance, as it laps a relatively easy comp.

How much are we paying for this high-single-digit revenue growth, mid-teens EPS growth, industry leader?

At $5,504 a share, Booking trades at 25 times forward earnings, and 22 times '26 estimates. These estimates are in-line with the company's outlook.

Notably, these valuation levels are relatively high for Booking, which historically trades in the 20x-23x range, in line with the market. In previous articles I argued that Booking should be getting a premium, and it seems like it finally does.

However, I think that for a company that's growing earnings at a mid-teens pace like clockwork, fair valuation is still a good enough entry point.

Conclusion

Booking is quietly running away with the prize of becoming the undisputed go-to spot to book travel. Fuelled by its best-in-class loyalty offerings, immediate customer service, and unparalleled supply of options, Booking gains market share from quarter to quarter, while improving margins.

In my view, no other option comes close to Booking for investors who want to capitalize on the travel industry. Despite a fair valuation, I expect Booking's mid-teens earnings growth to support market-beating returns long into the future.

As such, I reiterate Booking at a 'Buy'.

data.title: Fastenal: Just Too High A Price To Pay
data.description:
Kenio Fontes co-authored this article

Fastenal (NASDAQ:FAST) is an industrial manufacturer that has made its business attractive from a profitability standpoint over its tenure on the stock market; this is the major selling point for FAST, that it makes money.

They don't have a fancy AI-driven story plastered all over their IR website; they make fasteners, and industrial equipment, and do so in a "lean" way, to keep costs down and profitability up.

I don't particularly like companies that don't make money (some of you do, tsk tsk), and so when I see FAST, I see a firm standing out from a sea of "hot investments" that, from where I'm sitting, look like firepits for investors' cash.

FAST stock is a component of both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100, although at a fairly low weight due to its sub-$50B market cap. It has kept up with the S&P 500 over the last decade in returns, however, and has its cycles somewhat independent of the market; such is the fate of industrial firms.

Fastenal's Brighter Side

The chart below shows what I'm talking about, with FAST being a profitable company, the consequence of being a year of being a resilient firm with growing revenues, good margins, and cash generation. It's consistent and growing EPS and dividend per share, which is made even better by the smaller number of outstanding shares, which has been shrinking on the whole over the last decade, although up from a low in 2023.

This is only possible thanks to a very interesting and differentiated business model. FAST's model is all about helping companies be "lean," which is corporate speak for paying less to get more value for the business.

The Gillette Stadium case study shows that Fastenal's vending machine products, systems, and services make it possible to know where the tools are, but also to have more control and information about how they are being used, the quantity, and so on. This has an impact on time savings, but also on financials, helping with working capital and potential cost savings.

That's why Fastenal's trends are good. The more value the company can add, the greater the potential to maintain this constant growth in revenue, with not only more and more companies using it but also the companies that already use it, using it more and allowing for cross-selling, up-sell and/or eventual price increases due to the moat and need for these services.

There is a premium one can command by being a flagship partner for niches like stadium operations. Every industry has "default" partner organizations, firms that just make sense to do business with because their reputation is so positive. This allows for the stock to be bought up more, and investors consider it "goodwill."

It's not enough to make FAST a buy.

The Pricing… The Elephant in the Room

Estimates point to revenue growing in the high single digits over the next few years, with EPS being slightly higher than that.

But the challenge lies in valuation (and that's why it receives a D- for valuation in SA's Quant Ratings). This is good growth, and as Fastenal has a strong moat, it could allow compounding in the long term. But compounding is certainly limited by the current stock price. Even if EPS grows at the high single digits every year, in 2027 the price-to-earnings ratio would still be above 30x, greatly reducing the earnings yield and dividend potential.

I prefer to avoid it for now because of this limited potential. There's not so much room for accelerated growth since the multiples already price this in. There's also no room for multiple re-rating since it already incorporates the company's quality. And because of the expensive price, the shareholder yield is also limited.

The firm carries a high PE 5 (5-year smoothed PE) in particular, signaling that it is relatively expensive now compared to its last few years.

So, Sell FAST?

Not so fast. (See what I did there?) It's not that easy. The positive story that FAST brings to the table, and its success case study, the moat, etc., all still matter. This stock could be a winner still, so I wouldn't shun it from a portfolio.

This is where my risk management skills have to come into play; I publish so many of these "hold" articles because it's rare I see more reward than risk in a given situation in modern markets. There is always a reason for a stock to go up, and FAST is a stock that has a lot of reasons. But when markets fall, even if FAST is doing well on its own, it tends to take everything with it. We saw that in April this year. Everything has beta.

Will it outperform SPX? What about the sector (XLI)?

It's hard to say. Even if the companies continue to grow at their current pace, the market may not reward it. A lot of that powerful growth and moat story is priced in, and I don't see the entry point currently that makes FAST worthwhile for my portfolio right now.

The gut-check reaction to the chart is no. The setup isn't there. To borrow a Trumpism, FAST just doesn't have the cards.

Maybe they'll surprise me, and I hope they do.

Conclusion

Fastenal (FAST) is a great company that has given investors solid returns in the past, keeping up with the flagship benchmark it is a part of today. However, the clouds of valuation hang high over its head, and that could muddy future returns for investors, who are reliant on the company not meeting expectations, but beating them. The price is just too high on this one for me to walk into it, and the chart doesn't look good enough to take a swing at it.

If one was going to take that swing, I'd say they should handle it like I did Bristol-Myers Squibb, with a "dog collar." This is a stock I'd also want to limit my downside on, just in case.
